name: Number and Shape Patterns — Find the Rule
whatIsIt: A pattern follows a RULE. Some patterns REPEAT (▲■▲■). Some GROW by the same jump (3, 6, 9, 12 — add 3). Some shrink (20, 16, 12 — subtract 4). Once you find the rule, you can extend the pattern forwards or even backwards, and fill in a missing middle term.
whyNeeded: Spotting rules is the heart of maths — it's how we predict, how times tables work, and how tricky problems become easy. Patterns in numbers, shapes and even calendars all use the same 'find the rule' skill.
realLifeUses: Calendar dates jump by 7 each week
Border and rangoli designs repeat a unit
Stair-step brick designs grow by a fixed amount

vocabulary.meaning: What the pattern does each step (repeat a unit, or add/subtract a fixed number).
Continue the pattern using the rule.

story.title: The Missing Step
story.text: Miss Rao writes 4, 8, ?, 16, 20 on the board and asks Dev for the missing number. Dev checks the jumps: 8 − 4 = 4, and 20 − 16 = 4. 'The rule is add 4 each time,' he says. 'So after 8 comes 8 + 4 = 12.' He fills in 12 and the row is perfect.
story.extractedProblem: In 4, 8, ?, 16, 20, what is the missing number?

visual.caption: Jumps of 4: 4, 8, 12, 16, 20. Find the jump, then fill the gap.

teachingGallery.title: Repeat, grow, or shrink
teachingGallery.note: Repeating patterns have a unit; number patterns have a jump.

standardMethod.summary: Find the rule (repeating unit, or the jump), then extend or fill the gap.
steps: Check if it repeats (a unit like ▲■) or changes by a number.
For number patterns, find the jump between two pairs to be sure.
Add the jump to go forwards, subtract it to go backwards.
For a missing middle term, apply the jump to the term before it.

commonMistakes.mistake: Deciding the jump from just one pair, so an odd sequence is misread.
commonMistakes.fix: Check the jump on at least two pairs before trusting it, then apply it consistently.

commonMistakes.mistake: Adding when the pattern is shrinking (or subtracting when it is growing).
commonMistakes.fix: Notice if numbers get bigger (add) or smaller (subtract) and keep that direction.

challenge.q: Aarti sees 2, 4, 8, 16 and says 'add 2 each time'. What's really happening?

options.label: It DOUBLES each time (×2), so next is 32

challenge.answer: It DOUBLES each time (×2), so next is 32
hintLadder: The jump is not the same — it doubles.

questions.q: To be sure of a number pattern's rule you should…

options.label: Check the jump on two pairs

questions.answer: Check the jump on two pairs

teachBackPrompt: Make up two patterns for a grown-up — one that grows and one that shrinks — and leave a gap in the middle of each. Ask them to fill the gaps, then explain the rule you used.

revisionCard.summary: Find the rule: a repeating unit, or a jump (add/subtract the same number). Check the jump on two pairs. Add to go forward, subtract to go back. For a missing middle term, apply the jump to the term before it.

rememberIt.hook: Growing patterns add a bit each time - count the change
rememberIt.unpack: Match shapes to the term number. 1,3,5 (odd) or 1,3,6,10 (triangular). Find the rule linking position to value.

studentQuestions.q: How do I link the shape to its number?
studentQuestions.a: Count the objects at each step and see how the count grows with the position - that is the rule.
Dots that make triangles: 1,3,6,10... each adds the next counting number.
